coredata.dc:title: Lychee-like SnO2 hollow microspheres sensitized by carbon quantum dots for high-sensitivity ethanol sensor
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Yuan, Zhenyu
Sui, Yongchen
Ding, Meige
Zhu, Hongmin
coredata.dc:description: In this work, the application of carbon quantum dots as known as CQDs for ethanol detection is pioneered, and a multi-fold improvement in gas sensor performance is achieved through the unique electron transport mechanism of carbon quantum dots. The lychee-like SnO2 hollow microspheres and CQDs are prepared by a solvent heat method. The composites are assembled by rapid grinding, ultrasonication, and stirring. The minimum detection limit is 500 ppb. The response/recovery time of the best mass percentage composite is 9 s/16 s to ethanol at 100 ppm with an optimum working temperature of 216℃ in the target gas-sensitive test. Such a fast response speed improvement is ascribed to the excellent electron transfer efficiency of the 5 nm diameter carbon quantum dots (CQDs).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cqds
Sno2
Hollow microspheres
Π-states

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of sno2 hollow microspheres, CQDs, and CQDs@SnO2 composites

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD fraction of pure SnO2 and XRD fraction of CQDs@SnO2 with different mass percentages.
(a), (b), (c) pure SnO2 SEM images, (d) 0.5wt% CQDs@SnO2 composite TEM transmission electron microscopy images. (e), (f) TEM transmission electron microscopy images of CQDs.

XPS spectra of (a) survey spectrum, (b) Sn 3d, (c) C1s, and (d) O1s of 0.5 wt%CQDs@SnO2 composites, (e), (f) UV–Vis DRS spectrum of 0.5wt% CQDs@SnO2 composites, (g) FTIR spectra of materials with different ratios.

(a) Gas response of sensors to 100 ppm ethanol at different temperatures, (b) Ra of sensors based on different ratios of sensitive materials with the increasing of temperature, (c) Response and recovery time of pure SnO2 and best mass percentages of 0.5wt% CQDs@SnO2 composites at 216 °C.

(a) Response-recovery time curves and fitting curve of 0.5wt% CQDs@SnO2 composites for detecting ethanol at different concentrations, (b) Response function of the sensor for different concentrations of ethanol.

(a) Four responses of 0.5wt% CQDs@SnO2 sensor in 100 ppm ethanol environment, (b)comparison of the selectivity of pure SnO2 and 0.5wt% CQDs@SnO2 for different gases with concentrations of 100 ppm, (c) long-term stability of the 0.5wt% CQDs@SnO2 sensor, (d) Cross-selectivity of 0.5wt% CQDs@SnO2 sensors in multiple gas mixtures.
Variation of the response of the pure SnO2 sensor and the 0.5wt% CQDs@SnO2 sensor to 100 ppm ethanol gas in the relative humidity RH22.8%−60% range, respectively.
(a) Large Π-bond in benzene ring and graphene molecular structure, (b) carbon quantum dot molecular structure, (c) the electronic transition in CQDs.
Performance of the Ethanol sensors based on different sensing materials.

head.ce:title: Lychee-like SnO2 hollow microspheres sensitized by carbon quantum dots for high-sensitivity ethanol sensor

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Information Science and Engineering, Northeastern University, Shenyang 110819, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Information Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Information Science and Engineering, Northeastern University, Shenyang 110819, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, the application of carbon quantum dots as known as CQDs for ethanol detection is pioneered, and a multi-fold improvement in gas sensor performance is achieved through the unique electron transport mechanism of carbon quantum dots. The lychee-like SnO2 hollow microspheres and CQDs are prepared by a solvent heat method. The composites are assembled by rapid grinding, ultrasonication, and stirring. The minimum detection limit is 500 ppb. The response/recovery time of the best mass percentage composite is 9 s/16 s to ethanol at 100 ppm with an optimum working temperature of 216℃ in the target gas-sensitive test. Such a fast response speed improvement is ascribed to the excellent electron transfer efficiency of the 5 nm diameter carbon quantum dots (CQDs).

ce:list-item.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: VOCs such as methanol, acetone, and ethanol commonly exist in human production and life. A large number of VOCs in the air can be a severe health hazard for the human body [1–3]. For example, ethanol is used in places such as the chemical industry, agricultural production, and traffic safety [4,5]. However, humans may suffer from neurotoxic damage or fatty liver degeneration if exposed to ethanol for prolonged periods [6], which may also lead to acute toxic asphyxiation in severe cases. Thus, it is urgent to develop ethanol sensors with faster response time, higher response, environment friendly, and more stability to detect ethanol quickly and effectively.

SnO2 [7–9] and ZnO [10] are common inorganic sensor materials for VOC detection. Pure metal oxide semiconductors have been decorated by various methods to enhance their performance in multiple aspects, such as synthetic porous structures [5,11], noble metals doping [12–14], construction of n-n or n-p heterojunctions [15–18], and quantum dot modifications [8,19–21]. In addition to inorganic materials, the research on organic semiconductor materials is also excellent. For example, S. Ali et al. prepared a variety of NDI-based electrochemical sensors through an intensive study of NDI to achieve high-performance detection of ammonia gas at room temperature [22]. For example, the NDI-A type sensor has achieved excellent sensing performance in high-humidity environments [23]. The NDI-CN4 sensor's performance was ascribed to the low-lying LUMO level, where the strong electron-withdrawing cyano groups were attached to the NDI core [24]. Materials in this paper are not as resistant to humidity as organic materials but still have the advantages of easy synthesis and easy modification.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are materials supported by a carbon skeleton, with a suitable band gap and abundant functional groups caused by sp2 hybridized carbon and other amorphous carbon. In addition, because of the low-cost and wide range of raw materials of CQDs, which can be obtained by pyrolyzing any carboxylic acid [25], no metal contamination and non-toxic, and its conjugated Π structure brings efficient and fast electron transfer ability, CQDs have been widely used in photocatalysis [26–29], solar cells [30], and lithium batteries [31]. But, CQDs are rarely reported in the field of gas sensing. Carbone [19] formed a composite material modified with carbon quantum dots based on NiO, which reduced the response/recovery time and exhibited high sensitivity to methane; Yu et al. enhanced the sensitivity of the sensor to NO by combining carbon quantum dots with ZnO to form a composite material [20]. The above works illustrated that CQDs possess unique properties that can be effectively applied in gas sensing.

In order to apply the efficient electron transfer capability of CQDs to gas sensors to investigate more environmentally friendly and higher-performance quantum dot-type ethanol sensors, in this paper, we synthesized SnO2 hollow microspheres to provide a larger surface area for the absorption of carbon quantum dots. The SnO2 decorated with carbon quantum dots resulted in a new composite material with superior electron transport capability [32]. Compared to recently reported ethanol sensors, the sensor in this paper has a higher response [33] and faster recovery time at a lower operating temperature [34–36]. The innovations of this paper are as follows: (a) Due to the excellent electron transfer ability of CQDs, the response speed of the composite is further enhanced. (b) The unique “lychee” structure of CQDs@SnO2 can maximize the specific surface area and provide more gas contact area [37]. (c) Pioneering application of CQDs used in solar cell field to ethanol gas sensing, providing a direction for studying a low-cost, environmentally friendly quantum dot-type gas sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of sno2 hollow microspheres, CQDs, and CQDs@SnO2 composites
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 1 , 12 ml DMF, and 40 mmol NaOH were added to 48 ml DI water with 4.00 mmol SnCl4–5H2O under stirring for 30 min. The mixed solution was heated in a 100 ml reaction kettle at 160 °C for 16 h. After centrifugal washing, dry the product for 24 h at 80 °C, and finally, continuously calcined the product in an air environment of 400° for 1 hour to obtain SnO2 hollow microspheres. 0.4 g ascorbic acid and 4 ml ethylene glycol were mixed in 6 ml DI water and stirred for 30 min. The mixed solution was heated in a reaction kettle for 70 min at 160 °C to obtain a yellow carbon quantum dot (CQDs) solution.

The SnO2 material obtained was mechanically ground for 10 min, dissolved in anhydrous ethanol, then a certain amount of CQDs was dropped into the solution with stirring and reacted for 10 min with the help of ultrasonication. The CQDs@SnO2 composites were centrifugal washed to obtain CQDs@SnO2 composites. To test the performance of the composites more accurately, a control group modified by different concentrations of CQDs was prepared for more rigorous experiments.

Testing is performed on a gas test system in the laboratory as shown in Fig. 2 . The Acetone (99%), Triethylamine (99%), Dimethylbenzene (99%), HCHO (37%), and Ethanol (99.7%) gases required in the experiment are all volatile gases. For the test, the volatile saturated gas in the liquid bottle of the corresponding volume of gas was extracted using a syringe and injected into the gas chamber through the injection port. Make sure the gas chamber is closed when injecting. The temperature is controlled by a Ni-Cr heating wire in the sensor. A certain amount of CQDs@SnO2 composite was dispersed into anhydrous ethanol and stirred, then transferred material onto the sensor with a pipette gun.

The sample's phase structure, including elemental composition and chemical state, was investigated by the XRD-7000 X-ray diffraction (XRD, Cu-Kα radiation, λ = 1.5406 Å) from Shimadzu. The composites’ microstructure and morphology were observed by S-4800 SEM from Hitachi. The material's microstructure was investigated by TEM. The energy band gap of the optimally proportioned composites was characterized using UV–visible DRS, and the materials’ functional groups were tested by FTIR.

The test was performed by injecting an equal amount of gas into a closed test gas chamber using a syringe. N-type gas sensors’ response can be obtained by:
(1)
S =
R a
R g
where Rg and Ra represent the resistance value in the target gas and synthetic air, respectively.

The XRD study was performed by Shimadzu XRD-7000 (XRD, Cu-Kα radiation, λ = 1.5406 Å) instrument at 10°- 80° of 2θ angle. As shown in Fig. 3 , the SnO2 material with distinct peaks marked at 26.6°, 33.9°, 37.9°, and 51.7°, corresponding to diffraction planes (110), (101), (200), and (211), respectively, demonstrating that the SnO2 material was successfully fabricated (ICSD-41–1445). There are no obvious peaks of CQDs in the samples because the content of CQDs is too low. The CQDs will be observed in the TEM. As the concentration of CQDs increases, the peaks of the samples gradually decrease, which is due to the modification of CQDs, resulting in the symmetry of the crystal structure being affected and crystal defects. The higher the concentration of the CQDs, the greater the affected [21,26]. Fig. 4 (a) shows the structure of the surface morphology of SnO2, which clearly shows that the SnO2 was a standard microsphere with a diameter of about 4 μm. The enlarged portion of Fig. 4(b) shows that the SnO2 is hollow inside. Fig. 4(c) shows that the surface of SnO2 is granular, which is effective for increasing the surface area of the material. To better observe the microstructure and morphology of the composites, TEM observations were performed for CQDs and CQDs@SnO2, respectively. Fig. 4(d) demonstrates the presence of CQDs with a diameter of ∼5 nm on the surface of SnO2. Fig. 4(e) shows the TEM images of multiple CQDs, and it can be seen that most of them have a diameter between 2 and 5 nm, which is consistent with the definition of quantum dots with a diameter generally less than 10 nm. Fig. 4(f) shows the lattice stripe spacing of quantum dots is ∼0.2 nm, which is approximately the same as the (002) distance of crystalline graphite [38]. The 0.5wt% CQDs@SnO2 was further investigated for its elemental composition and chemical state using XPS and obtained the spectrum. Fig. 5 (a) proves that the composite contains elements of Sn, O, and C. Two peaks in Fig. 5(b) marked at 487.28 eV and 495.58 eV are brought by the energies of Sn4+ [21]. The functional groups of -CH and -CO are determined by analysing the peaks of 285.4 eV and 286.9 eV [39] in Fig. 5(c). The 531.0 eV and 532.3 eV peaks in Fig. 5(d) can be identified as O2− in SnO2 [40] and oxygen in hydroxyl groups. Fig. 5(e), (f) shows the UV–Vis DRS absorption spectra of 0.5wt% CQDs@SnO2 composites, the band energy can be obtained as follows.
(2)
α h v = A
(
h v −
E g
)
2
where A and h are constant, respectively. The α is expressed as the absorption coefficient, and the υ is the spectral frequency.

Fig. 5(g) shows the FTIR spectra of sensor materials with different ratios. The absorption peak located at 3430.36 cm−1 indicates -OH, -CH stretching vibrations for the conjugation of multiple molecules, and another absorption peak located at ∼1650 cm−1 indicates C = C stretching vibrations for the multiple molecules and conjugated Π-bonds. The FTIR spectrum of 0.1wt% CQDs@SnO2 is similar to the FTIR spectrum of pristine SnO2, this is due to the low concentration of CQDs. When the concentration of CQDs is 0.5wt% or more, the vibrations of -CH and -OH bonds become significantly stronger, which proves the rich functional group property of CQDs.

Fig. 6 (a) shows the pure SnO2 and the composites doped with different mass percentages were investigated experimentally under different temperature conditions. Semiconductor sensors require a certain operating temperature. The operating temperature will bring a certain power consumption, which is difficult to avoid, but is acceptable, many previous reports also use the operating temperature between 200 ℃ to 300 ℃ [36,41–43]. Energy consumption can be reduced by optimizing the heating system, or by changing the structure to reduce the operating temperature to reduce energy consumption [44]. The sensitive material was covered onto the sensor using the drop coating method, and the formed films ranged from 10 μm to 30 μm [45,46]. In addition, since the sensor in this study is a surface-controlled sensor based on the surface response, the effect on performance brought by the film thickness is acceptable within the above-acceptable film thickness range. Fig. 6(b) shows the Ra of sensors with different ratios of sensitive materials. At the same temperature conditions, response to ethanol reveals an increasing and then decreasing trend as the concentration of CQDs increases. It can be explained that the modification of the appropriate amount of CQDs can enlarge the materials’ specific surface area and thicken heterogeneous junctions, so the resistance will change. As the concentration of CQDs increases, quantum dot aggregation occurs, and the resistance of the material is affected, performance will also be reduced. The reaction time including response and recovery of the pure SnO2 material sensor compared with the 0.5 wt% CQDs@SnO2 composite can be acquired from Fig. 6(c). Within the scope of Gas "ON", The response time is reduced to 1/4 of the original, at 9 s, and the recovery time is reduced at 16 s.

As shown in Fig. 7 (a), the response value of the optimal ratio CQDs@SnO2 varies with the increase in ethanol gas concentration. The fitted equation was derived as S = 1.28122+0.26772C. The S and C express the response, and concentration, respectively. And the excellent linearity of the sensor response to the gas concentration gradient is verified by fitting the curve. Fig. 7(b) shows the variation of the resistance value of the sensor for different concentrations of ethanol environment. To emphasize the advantages of the sensors in this study, the CQDs@SnO2 sensor was compared with other ethanol gas sensors in terms of performance and the results are organized in Table 1 . The sensor in this work can provide a higher response to ethanol [8,47–50] with less response/recovery time without the need for high operating temperatures [47,50]. As shown in Fig. 8 (a), the sensor was tested four times under the same test environment for repeatability, and relatively consistent curves were obtained. Fig. 8(b) shows the selectivity of the pure SnO2 and the best mass percentage CQDs@SnO2 composites for different species of gases at 100 ppm. The stability of the optimal mass percentage CQDs@SnO2 is demonstrated in Fig. 8(c), and the response is dynamically balanced. It can be explained that the high chemical inertness of the carbon material itself [51], makes the composite possess excellent chemical stability. Fig. 8(d) shows the response of 0.5 wt% CQDs@SnO2 to different gas mixtures. The response to 100 ppm ethanol is defined as the standard value, and the responses value to triethylamine at a doping concentration of 20 ppm, formaldehyde at 20 ppm, and acetone at 20 ppm are 31.5, 30.8, and 29, respectively. This variation in response illustrates that the response of the 0.5 wt% CQDs@SnO2 sensor is affected by other gases, but not significantly.

The response variation of sensors covered with two materials with the RH in the range of 22.8%−60% are shown in Fig. 9 . After the relative humidity setting, the temperature value of the test environment is 25 °C. The gas sensors of both materials showed a tendency to decrease in response as the relative humidity was increased. The reason may be: firstly, the increase in humidity will cause water molecules to adsorb on the surface of the gas sensor, compete with the gas molecules to be measured for adsorption sites, thus reducing the interaction between the gas molecules to be measured and the sensor surface, resulting in a decrease in the response of the sensor. Secondly, elevated humidity affects the airflow, which slows down the diffusion rate of gas molecules, thus making it take longer for the gas molecules to reach the sensor surface, resulting in a lower response of the sensor. The difference is that the decline of the composite with the best ratio is slightly larger. This is since in CQDs@SnO2 composites, the more polar hydrophilic groups such as carboxyl groups can bind to water molecules, leading to changes in the properties of the material surface and thus a reduced response to the test gas [52].

Resistive modulation is the mechanism of action of CQDs@SnO2 composites. Adsorbed oxygen was formed by O2 adsorbs from air to the surface of CQDs@SnO2 composites as shown in equations [53,54].
(3)
O
2
(
g a s
)
→
O
2
(
a d s
)
(
Room
temperature
(4)
O
2
(
a d s
)
+
e
−
→
O 2
−
(
a d s
)
( T
<
150
∘
C )
(5)
O 2
−
(
a d s
)
+
e
−
→ 2
O
(
a d s
)
−
( 150
∘
C
<
T
<
400
∘
C )
(6)
O
(
a d s
)
−
+
e
−
→
O
(
a d s
)
2 −
( T
>
400
∘
C )

Oxygen is attached to the material surface, and by reaction, electrons which were originally stayed in the conduction band are transferred into oxygen, after which depletion layers are formed between the different materials, resulting in higher resistance of the material, as shown in Fig. 10 . This work's ideal operating temperature for testing the best mass percentage CQDs@SnO2 composite is 216 °C. From Eq. (5), the adsorbed oxygen is mostly O −, and when exposed to ethanol, the reactions occur as follows [6,54,55].
(7)
C 2
H 5
O H + 6
O
−
→ 2 C
O 2
+ 3
H 2
O + 6
e
−

When the adsorbed oxygen is taken away by ethanol, the electrons released are reintroduced into the composite and restored in the conduction band, causing the barrier to narrow to weaken the resistance.

As shown in Fig. 11 (a), the presence of Π-bonds in the graphene [56] as well as in the CQDs allows more electrons to be excited into the conduction band, enhancing the conductivity of CQDs@SnO2 and reducing the response time [57,58]. Fig. 11(b) shows the presence of -CO and -OH functional groups in CQDs, which correspond to the XPS and FTIR results. The electronic transition in Fig. 11(c) further explains theoretically the jumping of electrons from the n state to the Π* state inside the CQDs@SnO2 material, and the reduced band gap makes the electron migration faster [59,60].

In summary, a hollow spherical SnO2 material was prepared by a simple synthesis method and assembled into composites using non-toxic and low-cost CQDs of 5 nm diameter for modification. The application of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) for gas detection of ethanol is pioneered. A fast response time of 9 s is achieved by the unique electron transfer efficiency of carbon quantum dots, and the minimum detection limit is 500 ppb. Providing a method for the future development of quantum dot-bound gas sensors with high performance.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (61973058, 62033002, 61833006, and 62071112).
